perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed and interpreted in accordance with the law of the Republic of South Africa.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed and interpreted in accordance with the law as determined by Aldes.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the application of South African law and replaces it with a clause giving Aldes the power to determine which law applies. This could violate consumer protection laws in South Africa.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows Aldes to bypass South African consumer protection laws, which mandate that contracts be governed by fair and just laws, giving consumers specific rights and protections. Section 4(4) of the Consumer Protection Act stipulates that any terms that directly aim to defeat the purpose of the Act or deceive the consumer are prohibited.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates governance by the law of the Republic of South Africa. The changed text allows Aldes to determine which law applies, potentially circumventing South African consumer protection laws. The Perturbation Explanation states that this violates consumer protection laws in South Africa. The Law Explanation says that the modified text allows Aldes to bypass these laws, which mandate fair and just laws and give consumers specific rights. While there are no law snippets, the change directly contradicts the legally mandated protection by giving a private entity the power to decide what law governs, effectively removing a legally mandated consumer protection, which aligns with the definition of an 'Omission Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Any indulgence, latitude or extension of time which may be allowed by either of Aldes, the Seller/s and/or the Business/es shall not under any circumstances be taken as preventing any of them from exercising their rights contained in this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Any indulgence, latitude or extension of time which may be allowed by either of Aldes, the Seller/s and/or the Business/es shall be taken as preventing any of them from exercising their rights contained in this Agreement .
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the meaning of this clause creates a legal contradiction, because now if Aldes allows a delay, they forfeit their rights. This could be argued as unfair and unconscionable, violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Consumer Protection Act, Section 40
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 40 of the Consumer Protection Act prohibits unconscionable conduct. The changed text creates a situation where a simple act of leniency results in the waiver of rights, which may be deemed unreasonably harsh and unfair, thus potentially violating Section 40 of the CPA.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation indicates that the reversed clause could be deemed unfair and unconscionable, potentially violating consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that waiving rights due to leniency could be unreasonably harsh. While no law snippets were provided to confirm this, the changes to the contract removes the original protection that Aldes had to not forfeit their rights if they show leniency. The Changed Text directly contradicts the Original Text by now preventing Aldes from exercising their rights if they grant any indulgence. This undermines their original protection and could be deemed unconscionable. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: As such, the Buyer warrants and guarantees that all information presented and p rovided via and in terms of the Buyer’s Profile was, is and will remain true, complete and accurate and that it will immediately inform Aldes should there be any change in t he information provided/presented.
perturbation.changed_text: As such, the Buyer warrants and guarantees that all information presented and p rovided via and in terms of the Buyer’s Profile was, is true, complete and accurate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and that it will immediately inform Aldes should there be any change in the information provided/presented' removes the buyer's obligation to update their information. This could violate financial regulations if the buyer's financial status changes significantly, impacting their ability to complete the transaction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Financial Intelligence Centre Act, 38 of 2001 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 21 of FICA requires accountable institutions (which could include Aldes in certain circumstances) to keep customer information up to date. By removing the buyer's obligation to inform Aldes of changes, the agreement makes it more difficult for Aldes to comply with its FICA obligations related to customer due diligence and risk assessment, especially concerning anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing.

The tables below are for the entire Public Law. Pub. L. Section Status USC Title USC Section Authorizes TOPN: Table of Popular Names Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a clause where the buyer is obligated to inform Aldes of any changes to their information. The changed text omits this obligation. The Law Explanation states this could make it difficult for Aldes to comply with FICA obligations related to customer due diligence, particularly concerning anti-money laundering. The omission of this obligation directly contradicts the intent of maintaining up-to-date customer information as potentially required by FICA, creating a legal risk for Aldes. Even though Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 does not mention FICA directly, the Law Explanation's reference to FICA, combined with the Perturbation Explanation regarding financial regulations, suggests the omission could lead to non-compliance, therefore contradicting potential legal requirements.
